title: Apply: First Amendment: Assembly and Petition
description: Apply your understanding of the Assembly and Petition Clauses of the First Amendment with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Apply: First Amendment: Assembly and Petition

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the opinion of the Supreme Court of Kansas. The court was considering a city [[☃ definition 1]] that prohibited parading without city approval.**

We do not believe that the legislative grant of power to the city council, as [[☃ definition 2]] in the sections above cited, can be so [[☃ definition 3]] as to authorize the city council to take from the people of a city and the surrounding country a privilege exercised by them in every locality throughout the land—to form their processions and parade the streets with banners, music, songs, and shouts. It is an abridgment of the rights of the people. It [[☃ definition 4]] associated effort and action. It discourages united effort to attract public attention, and challenge public examination and criticism of the associated purposes. It discourages unity of feeling and expression on great public questions, economic, religious, and political. It practically destroys these great public demonstrations that are the most natural product of common aims and kindred purposes.

**Based on the excerpt, the Court found that the city ordinance violated which civil liberty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The freedom of assembly
The right to petition
The freedom of speech
The right to bear arms

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the opinion of the Supreme Court of Kansas. The court was considering a city [[☃ definition 1]] that prohibited parading without city approval.**

We do not believe that the legislative grant of power to the city council, as [[☃ definition 2]] in the sections above cited, can be so [[☃ definition 3]] as to authorize the city council to take from the people of a city and the surrounding country a privilege exercised by them in every locality throughout the land—to form their processions and parade the streets with banners, music, songs, and shouts. It is an abridgment of the rights of the people. It [[☃ definition 4]] associated effort and action. It discourages united effort to attract public attention, and challenge public examination and criticism of the associated purposes. It discourages unity of feeling and expression on great public questions, economic, religious, and political. It practically destroys these great public demonstrations that are the most natural product of common aims and kindred purposes.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The ordinance violates freedom of speech, as it does not allow people to voice their opinions without government interference.
The ordinance does not violate any individual rights, as it places limits on an activity that is not protected in the Constitution.
The ordinance limits public assemblies and undermines the people’s ability to express themselves as a group.
The ordinance is a legal restriction, as local governments can use time, place, and manner restrictions to promote public safety.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Commentaries on the Laws of England* (1765) by William Blackstone. The work was extremely influential on the founders and American legal scholars during and  after the American Revolution. It shaped their understanding of legal principles and helped frame the legal foundation of the United States, particularly in establishing the importance of common law traditions in American legal systems.**

A third subordinate right of every Englishman is that of applying to the courts of justice for [[☃ definition 1]] of injuries. . . .

If there should happen any uncommon injury, or infringement of the rights beforementioned, which the ordinary course of law is too defective to reach, there still remains a fourth subordinate right [[☃ definition 2]] to every individual, namely, the right of petitioning the king, or either house of parliament, for the redress of grievances. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of correcting or making something right

choices.content: Every Englishman must use the court system to address violations of their rights, and the courts are then responsible for petitioning the king or Parliament.
Every Englishman has certain rights that cannot be taken away by the king or Parliament, and using the court system is the only way to protect those rights.
Every Englishman is responsible for examining laws passed by the king or Parliament to make sure their own rights are protected.
Every Englishman can appeal to the king or Parliament if the courts cannot address a violation of their rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Commentaries on the Laws of England* (1765) by William Blackstone. The work was extremely influential on the founders and American legal scholars during and  after the American Revolution. It shaped their understanding of legal principles and helped frame the legal foundation of the United States, particularly in establishing the importance of common law traditions in American legal systems.**

A third subordinate right of every Englishman is that of applying to the courts of justice for [[☃ definition 1]] of injuries. . . .

If there should happen any uncommon injury, or infringement of the rights beforementioned, which the ordinary course of law is too defective to reach, there still remains a fourth subordinate right [[☃ definition 2]] to every individual, namely, the right of petitioning the king, or either house of parliament, for the redress of grievances. . . .

**The ideas in this excerpt led to which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of correcting or making something right

choices.content: The adoption of the Articles of Confederation
The addition of the First Amendment
The formation of political parties
The ratification of the Treaty of Paris

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a speech delivered by Senator James Buchanan in 1839. In the 1830s, Congress used the gag rule to prevent discussions of petitions to abolish slavery. Buchanan is discussing one such petition.**

The next argument advanced by the gentleman is, that we are not bound to receive this petition, because to grant its prayer would be unconstitutional? In this argument I shall not touch the question, whether Congress possess[es] the power to abolish slavery in the District of Columbia or not. Suppose they do not, can the gentleman maintain the position, that we are authorized by the Constitution to refuse to receive a petition from the people, because we may deem the object of it unconstitutional? . . . 

The people are not constitutional lawyers; but they feel oppression, and know when they are [[☃ definition 1]]. They present their complaints to us in the form of a petition. I ask, by what authority can we refuse to receive it? They have a right to spread their wishes and their wants before us, and to ask for [[☃ definition 2]]. We are bound respectfully to consider their request; and the best answer which we can give them is, that they have not [[☃ definition 3]] us the power, under the Constitution of the United States, to grant them the relief which they desire. On any other principle we may first decide that we have no power over a particular subject, and then refuse to hear the petitions of the people in relation to it. We would thus place the constitutional right of our constituents to petition at the mercy of our own [[☃ definition 4]].

**Which statement best summarizes Buchanan’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: made to suffer because of violations of rights
the act of correcting or making something right

choices.content: Congress should not refuse to receive a petition, even if the people are asking for something that might be unconstitutional.
Congress is responsible for passing laws that are constitutional and should avoid hearing any petitions that ask Congress to violate the Constitution.
Congress has a responsibility to act on popular petitions, even if doing so would be unconstitutional.
Congress cannot pass any laws banning slavery, as slavery is protected under the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a speech delivered by Senator James Buchanan in 1839. In the 1830s, Congress used the gag rule to prevent discussions of petitions to abolish slavery. Buchanan is discussing one such petition.**

The next argument advanced by the gentleman is, that we are not bound to receive this petition, because to grant its prayer would be unconstitutional? In this argument I shall not touch the question, whether Congress possess[es] the power to abolish slavery in the District of Columbia or not. Suppose they do not, can the gentleman maintain the position, that we are authorized by the Constitution to refuse to receive a petition from the people, because we may deem the object of it unconstitutional? . . . 

The people are not constitutional lawyers; but they feel oppression, and know when they are [[☃ definition 1]]. They present their complaints to us in the form of a petition. I ask, by what authority can we refuse to receive it? They have a right to spread their wishes and their wants before us, and to ask for [[☃ definition 2]]. We are bound respectfully to consider their request; and the best answer which we can give them is, that they have not [[☃ definition 3]] us the power, under the Constitution of the United States, to grant them the relief which they desire. On any other principle we may first decide that we have no power over a particular subject, and then refuse to hear the petitions of the people in relation to it. We would thus place the constitutional right of our constituents to petition at the mercy of our own [[☃ definition 4]].

**Based on the excerpt, what was the goal of Buchanan’s speech?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: made to suffer because of violations of rights
the act of correcting or making something right

choices.content: To enforce the rule that Congress cannot discuss petitions about slavery
To persuade the Supreme Court to abolish slavery
To encourage Congress to consider petitions from abolitionist groups
To suggest that more people need to become constitutional lawyers

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Massachusetts state constitution, written in 1780. **

The people have a right, in an orderly and peaceable manner, to . . . give instructions to their representatives, and to request of the legislative body, by the way of addresses . . . or [[☃ definition 1]], [[☃ definition 2]] of the wrongs done them, and of the grievances they suffer.

**Which right did the Massachusetts constitution protect in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to assemble
The right to protest
The right to association
The right to petition

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which scenario is protected under the First Amendment’s freedom of assembly?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Scott organizes a parade to celebrate St. Patrick’s Day.
Joaquin encourages protestors on Capitol Hill to burn down the Capitol building to get their argument across to lawmakers.
Chelsea stands in a park, yelling about the president through a megaphone.
Francesca and her friends park their cars on a freeway to block traffic to protest a plan to build a new toll bridge.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the North Carolina Declaration of Rights, written in 1776.**

That the people have a right to assemble together to consult for their common good, to instruct their representatives, and to apply to the legislature for [[☃ definition 1]] of grievances.

**This is an excerpt from the Massachusetts state constitution, written in 1780.**

The people have a right, in an orderly and peaceable manner, to assemble to consult upon the common good; give instructions to their representatives; and to request of the legislative body, by the way of addresses, petitions, or [[☃ definition 2]], redress of the wrongs done them, and of the grievances they suffer.

**This is an excerpt from the New Hampshire Bill of Rights, written in 1784.**

The people have a right, in an orderly and peaceable manner, to assemble and consult upon the common good, give instructions to their representatives; and to request of the legislative body, by way of petition or remonstrance, redress of the wrongs done them, and of the grievances they suffer.

**These excerpts can be used to support which argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The states believed that the right to assembly could only be lawful if those assembled aimed to petition the government.
The states believed the right to assembly and the right to petition were closely linked.
The states supported protecting the right to petition, but they did not include any protections for the right to assemble.
The states believed that the right to assemble could not be restricted, even if the assembly turned to violence.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the North Carolina Declaration of Rights, written in 1776.**

That the people have a right to assemble together to consult for their common good, to instruct their representatives, and to apply to the legislature for [[☃ definition 1]] of grievances.

**This is an excerpt from the Massachusetts state constitution, written in 1780.**

The people have a right, in an orderly and peaceable manner, to assemble to consult upon the common good; give instructions to their representatives; and to request of the legislative body, by the way of addresses, petitions, or [[☃ definition 2]], redress of the wrongs done them, and of the grievances they suffer.

**This is an excerpt from the New Hampshire Bill of Rights, written in 1784.**

The people have a right, in an orderly and peaceable manner, to assemble and consult upon the common good, give instructions to their representatives; and to request of the legislative body, by way of petition or remonstrance, redress of the wrongs done them, and of the grievances they suffer.

**Based on these excerpts, which guarantee did these early state constitutions aim to establish?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The responsibility of elected representatives to instruct the people in the principles of fair and just government.
The right of citizens to meet peacefully and to communicate their needs and grievances to the government
The obligation of state governments to immediately enact any measure requested through petitions presented by assembled groups
The people’s right to assemble to ensure that citizens could formally and publicly charging government officials with crimes

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *DeJonge v. Oregon* (1937).**

It follows from these considerations that, consistent with the Federal Constitution, peaceable assembly for lawful discussion cannot be made a crime. The holding of meetings for peaceable political action cannot be [[☃ definition 1]]. Those who assist in the conduct of such meetings cannot be branded as criminals on that score. The question, if the rights of free speech and peaceable assembly are to be preserved, is not as to the [[☃ definition 2]] under which the meeting is held, but as to its purpose; not as to the relations of the speakers, but whether their [[☃ definition 3]] [[☃ definition 4]] the bounds of the freedom of speech which the Constitution protects. 

If the persons assembling have committed crimes elsewhere, if they have formed or are engaged in a [[☃ definition 5]] against the public peace and order, they may be [[☃ definition 6]] for their conspiracy or other violation of valid laws. But it is a different matter when the State, instead of prosecuting them for such offenses, seizes upon mere participation in a peaceable assembly and a lawful public discussion as the basis for a criminal charge.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: agreement to do something wrong or illegal

choices.content: State governments cannot restrict the right to assemble under any circumstance, even if people at the gathering commit violence.
State governments can restrict public assembly if the person organizing the assembly has committed a crime.
State governments have the right to restrict the right to assemble for prisoners in their custody to ensure public safety.
State governments generally cannot make it a crime for people to assemble peaceably, as doing so would violate their rights under the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *DeJonge v. Oregon* (1937).**

It follows from these considerations that, consistent with the Federal Constitution, peaceable assembly for lawful discussion cannot be made a crime. The holding of meetings for peaceable political action cannot be [[☃ definition 1]]. Those who assist in the conduct of such meetings cannot be branded as criminals on that score. The question, if the rights of free speech and peaceable assembly are to be preserved, is not as to the [[☃ definition 2]] under which the meeting is held, but as to its purpose; not as to the relations of the speakers, but whether their [[☃ definition 3]] [[☃ definition 4]] the bounds of the freedom of speech which the Constitution protects. 

If the persons assembling have committed crimes elsewhere, if they have formed or are engaged in a [[☃ definition 5]] against the public peace and order, they may be [[☃ definition 6]] for their conspiracy or other violation of valid laws. But it is a different matter when the State, instead of prosecuting them for such offenses, seizes upon mere participation in a peaceable assembly and a lawful public discussion as the basis for a criminal charge.

**Which statement best describes the impact of the Court’s ruling in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: agreement to do something wrong or illegal

choices.content: It created a set of criteria for state laws that sought to restrict assembly.
An individual’s right to assembly was protected at the state level of government.
It allowed states to decide who could participate in public gatherings.
Any laws restricting the rights of criminals to assemble were judged to be constitutional.
